coredata.dc:title: Preparation of multicolor carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal method and their functionalization applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Xingyuan
Li, Jianfeng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become a popular research topic because of their many unique virtues, especially the significance of tunable photoluminescence properties. However, the synthesis of multicolor CQDs is still at an exploratory stage, and there are many dilemmas that have not yet been well resolved, such as long-wavelength emission and solid-state luminescence. Here, we selected suitable carbon and dopant sources as precursors for the synthesis of CQDs through the rational design of the experiments in an aqueous system, and the as-prepared CQDs possessed blue, green, yellow and red emissions, with optimal emissions of approximately 475 nm, 530 nm, 580 nm, and 645 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the corresponding PLQYs were 30.13 %, 36.84 %, 39.16 % and 21.36 %, respectively. It was found that the redshift of the emission wavelengths can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the increase in the sp2 conjugate size and the surface states. To conquer the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) of CQDs and realize the solid-state luminescence of CQDs, we successfully prepared solid-state fluorescent films and solid-state phosphors. Finally, multicolor CQDs were successfully applied to prepare monochromatic and white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs). The adopted green synthesis technology can provide a good technical support for the future development of CQDs synthesis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Tunable emission
Synergistic effect
Solid state light emitting
Light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of multicolor carbon quantum dots
Possible emission and formation mechanism of multicolor carbon quantum dots
Application of multicolor carbon quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Fujian Bureau of Major Projects ( 2019H4018 ) funding

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of (a) B-CQDs, (b) G-CQDs, (c) Y-CQDs and (d) R-CQDs.
Particle size distributions of (e) B-CQDs, (f) G-CQDs, (g) Y-CQDs and (h) R-CQDs.
High-resolution spectrum of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs.
Absorption spectra, excitation spectra, and emission spectra of B-CQDs(a),G-CQDs(b),Y-CQDs(c) and R-CQDs(d); Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of B-CQDs(e),G-CQDs(f),Y-CQDs(g) and R-CQDs(h).
Possible fluorescence mechanisms and energy levels of the four CQDs are shown schematically.
Pictures of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs/WPU composite films under daylight (a) and UV (b); B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs/SiO2 composite powders under daylight (c–f) and UV (g–j).

(a–e) Emission spectrum of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, R-CQDs, and W-CQDs phosphor-based LEDs; (inset) respective optical pictures. (f–j) CIE color coordinates of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, R-CQDs, and W-CQDs phosphor-based LEDs.

head.ce:title: Preparation of multicolor carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal method and their functionalization applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xian, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become a popular research topic because of their many unique virtues, especially the significance of tunable photoluminescence properties. However, the synthesis of multicolor CQDs is still at an exploratory stage, and there are many dilemmas that have not yet been well resolved, such as long-wavelength emission and solid-state luminescence. Here, we selected suitable carbon and dopant sources as precursors for the synthesis of CQDs through the rational design of the experiments in an aqueous system, and the as-prepared CQDs possessed blue, green, yellow and red emissions, with optimal emissions of approximately 475 nm, 530 nm, 580 nm, and 645 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the corresponding PLQYs were 30.13 %, 36.84 %, 39.16 % and 21.36 %, respectively. It was found that the redshift of the emission wavelengths can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the increase in the sp2 conjugate size and the surface states. To conquer the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) of CQDs and realize the solid-state luminescence of CQDs, we successfully prepared solid-state fluorescent films and solid-state phosphors. Finally, multicolor CQDs were successfully applied to prepare monochromatic and white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs). The adopted green synthesis technology can provide a good technical support for the future development of CQDs synthesis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely studied because of their unique structural and optical behavior [1].They are widely used as a novel functional carbon nanomaterial in fluorescence sensor, environments monitory, bio-imaging and optoelectronic devices [1–5]. They are expected to be the ideal successors of semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescent dyes due to their wide sources, non-toxic/low toxicity, good biocompatibility, easy modification and functionalization, and environmental friendliness [6–10].After nearly two decades of research, CQDs with unique properties can be prepared either by exfoliation and oxidative cutting on macromolecular carbon materials or by dehydration, carbonization and cross-linking on small molecule materials [11–13].More importantly, the raw materials to be combined with them are no longer the traditional small molecule compounds, and the introduction of a large number of new materials and the adoption of new synthesizing methods have made the study of CQDs even more valuable [14–17].Among the many unique advantages, the tunable photoluminescence (PL) property attracts the most attention [18].In recent years, extensive attempts have been made to realize spot tuning of CQDs in the visible range, and several possible tuning mechanisms have been proposed [19–23].Currently, luminescent CQDs in the full spectral range have also been prepared for various applications [5,8,24–29]. Although all these works prepared tunable CQDs with excellent PL, they still require different reaction solvents, separation reagents, and cumbersome and time-consuming process. To our knowledge, rarely has it been reported to prepare multicolor CQDs in a single hydrothermal reaction system, free from the effect of toxic solvents and cumbersome isolation and purification, and to shift the optimal emission position of CQDs from blue to red under a single hydrothermal reaction condition, which is of significant for the CQDs usage and mechanistic clarification.

CQDs, as fluorescent materials with tunable PL, has been extensively used and researched in the field of illumination and display. Unfortunately, most CQDs exhibited aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in the solid state due to the excess resonance energy transfer or π-π direct interactions, which is a primary concern restricting the application of CQDs [7,30–32].To realize solid-state luminescence of CQDs, the most important method at present is to disperse and fix the CQDs in various solid matrices, such as starch, silica (SiO2), inorganic salts, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and mesoporous materials to increase the distance between CQDs by physical methods, to make the CQDs more uniformly dispersed, and to realize the purpose of the inherent fluorescence characteristics of the CQDs solution [33–37]. Although the above methods realized the solid-state luminescence of CQDs, the prepared CQDs phosphors had a large gap in particle size distribution and poor stability, which seriously limited the application areas of CQDs phosphors.SiO2 powder is a high-quality adsorbent material, commonly used in the separation and purification of compounds by column chromatography, it has a wealth of microporous structural, a high surface area, and high purity and activity [38–40]. Its unique micropore structure and high specific surfaces area offer a unique advantage for the uniform dispersion of CQDs, and at the same time create an opportunity for the hydrogen bonding adsorption of CQDs on the SiO2 interface, which is conducive to the realization of solid-state luminescence of CQDs.

In this work, we developed a simple and efficient method to prepare multicolor CQDs in a single hydrothermal system. The photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of the as-prepared blue CQDs (B-CQDs), green CQDs (G-CQDs), yellow CQDs (Y-CQDs), and red CQDs (R-CQDs) were 30.13 %, 36.84 %, 39.16 %, and 21.36 %, respectively. Most importantly, we prepared multicolor CQDs/SiO2 powder composite phosphors by a simple and efficient hydrogen bonding adsorption method, realizing solid-state powder luminescence of CQDs. Furthermore, due to many hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CQDs, we successfully dispersed the prepared CQDs in nonionic aqueous polyurethane, realizing the preparation of fluorescent membranes with CQDs. Finally, by taking advantage of the excellent fluorescence properties of CQDs, we successfully applied multicolor CQDs in light-emitting diodes (LEDs). The synthesis techniques employed in for the future synthesis of CQDs and their solid-state preparation can provide strong technical support.

Tyrosine, di-n-butylamine, ethylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine were obtained from China Aladdin Chemical Co. Concentrated sulfuric acid (98 wt%) was acquired from China National Pharmaceutical Chemical Reagent Co. The column silica powder was purchased from Qingdao Ocean Chromatographic Column Silica Powder. Nonionic waterborne polyurethane purchased from Shanghai Belt New Material Company, China.

Polychromatic CQDs of tyrosine and three different nitrogen-containing compounds were obtained in an aqueous system.0.1 g of tyrosine and 0.1 g of di-n-butylamine were dissolved in 20 ml of DI water and sonicated for 10 min, and then the resulting precursors were reacted for 6 h in an autoclave at 200 °C. The product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove by-products and impurities. The obtained samples were dialyzed with 0.22 μm dialysis bags for 24 h (water change every 6 h), and the dialysis bag solution was lyophilized to obtain solid B-CQDs powders, which were dissolved in ethanol for the next step of characterization and research. For G-CQDs, only ethylenediamine was used instead of di-n-butylamine; for Y-CQDs, only 0.05 g of o-phenylenediamine was used instead of 0.1 g of di-n-butylamine; and the reactive fluid was changed to a sparse Sulfuric Acid solution of 3.0 moL/L in comparison to that of Y-CQDs for R-CQDs.

20 mg of each of the four prepared CQDs were dissolved in 50 ml of ethanol,10 g of nonionic waterborne polyurethane was taken and added to 10 mL of the alcohol solution at the CQDs, dispersed by ultrasonic dispersal for 30 min, poured into a tetrafluoroethylene abrasive tool, and heated at 100 °C for 2 h.

The four colors of CQDs used in the preparation of fluorescent films were taken as 20 mL of alcohol solution, which was added into 20 g of silica gel powder of the chromatographic column (dried at 100 °C for 2 h to remove water), and ultrasonically dispersed for 2 h, and then dried at 60 °C for 3 h to obtain the CQDs-based solid phosphor.
Three milliliters of each of the four CQDs/WPU mixtures used to prepare the films were then dropped onto a 365 nm UV light chip and dried at 100 °C for 2 o'clock.

JEOL JEM 2100 F transmission electron microscope was used to observe the appearance and morphology of CQDs; XPS analyzer from Thermo Scientific K-Alpha was used to determine the elemental composition of CQDs; PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer was used to determine the composition of functional groups on the surface of CQDs; RF-5301PC (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to analyze the morphology, size, and distribution of CQDs. All optical properties of CQDs; (TU-1900, Perkin General, China) to characterize the UV absorption properties of CQDs; D/max2200PC X to analyze the crystallinity of CQDs. The optical spectra of all four CQDs were normalized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of multicolor carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructures and morphologies of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs were recognized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM pictures of the multicolored CQDs and their size distributions are displayed in Fig. 1 From Fig. 1a–d, it can be observed obviously that the prepared multicolored CQDs have a uniform distribution, indicating that the as-prepared CQDs are well-dispersed in the ethanol solution with no obvious agglomerates. In addition, the HRTEM figures in Fig. 1a–d (insets) show that the multicolored CQDs all have obvious lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is in agreement with that of graphitic carbon (100), indicating the prepared multicolored CQDs have a graphite-like structure [41–43].The particle size distributions of the synthesized multicolor CQDs are presented in Fig. 1e-1(h) for B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs having an average of about 2.26,2.34, 2.65 and 3.04 nm, respectively. It is obvious that the grain diameters of CQDs are gradually larger with the red-shift of the optimal luminescence wavenumber of the four CQDs, which might be attributed to the quantum size effect as one of the factors for the red-shift of the luminescence wavelength of the CQDs [44,45]. The crystal structures of four CQDs were determined by x-ray diffractometry (XRD). Fig. S1a shows the XRD the data for four CQDs, which all show a single broad ridge at 21°, which corresponds to the (002) crystal planes of the graphitic carbon. This indicates that all four CQDs contain graphitic carbon structures [46–48]. In addition, the FTIR spectra were used to analyze the apparent functional groups and chemical bonds of the four CQDs. In Fig. S1b, the peak at 3680 cm−1 may be induced by N–H stretching vibration, the peaks in the range of 3128∼3286 cm−1 may be from O–H stretching vibration, the significant absorption peak at 1720 cm−1 may be from the C=O stretching vibration absorption peak, the absorption peak at 1548 cm−1 may be from C=C stretching vibration in the benzene ring, and in addition, the absorption peak at 967 cm−1 may be from the C–H bending vibration. These results show that the four CQDs have the similar surface functional groups and chemical bonds. Importantly, the absorption peaks of N–H, O–H, and C–O in the four CQDs are gradually enhanced with the red-shift of the emission wavelength, which may be due to the formation of various surface states by various functional groups on the surfaces of the CQDs [49]. To better explain this phenomenon and further to characterize the chemistry and surface compositions of the four CQDs, we utilized X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to analyze the CQDs.

The full XPS spectra of the four CQDs are shown in Fig. S1c. It is clearly seen the signature peaks at 284.8, 399.7, and 531.1 eV correspond to carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), and oxygen (O 1s), which indicates that the four CQDs are composed of the same elements, and among them, R-CQDs contain the most oxygen, and their emission wavelengths shift to the low-energy region (Table S1) [50]. Fig. 2 shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of the four CQDs, in which all the C1s spectra can be convolved into three peaks of 284.6 (C=C/C–C), 285.5 (C–O/C–N), and 287.5 eV (C=O), and the percentage of the deconvoluted peak areas of the peaks are shown in Table S2, where the C=O content basically tends to increase with the redshift of the emission wavelength [51]. All the N1s spectra can be convolved into two peaks, 399.1 (pyridine N) and 399.7 eV (amide N), and the percentage of the deconvoluted peak area of each peak is shown in Table S3, which shows that the content of amide N increases from 41.21 % to 61.80 % with the redshift of the emission wavelength. All the O1s spectra can be convolved into two peaks, 530.2 (C=O) and 531.9 eV (C–O), and the percentage of the deconvolved peak area of each peak is shown in Table S3, which shows that the content of C=O basically tends to increase with the redshift of emission wavelength, with the red shift of emission wavelength, the C=O content increased from 35.40 % to 60.61 % [52]. The increase in the C=O bond content indicates an increase in the surface oxidation of the CQDs, which serves as a trapping center for excitons, resulting in surface-state correlated fluorescence [53]. The XPS results show that the N- and O-related surface states content on the surface gradually increases with the emission wavelength redshift, which is also consistent with the FT-IR results [54]. Thus, it can be inferred that this is the main factor for the mission wavelength redshift of CQDs from the effect of the N- and O-related surface states.

The optical properties of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs dispersed in ethanol solution were characterized by UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. As indicated in Fig. 3 a–d, all the four CQDs show strong absorption in the UV region. They exhibit absorption at 250–300 nm which can be attributed to the π-π* transitions of the C=C and C=N bonds in the aromatic ring structure [55]. The peaks of B-CQDs and G-CQDs at 300–400 nm peaks belong to the n-π* transitions of the C=O/C=N bonds of the surface functional groups [56,57]. Importantly, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs exhibit strong absorption peaks at 400–480 nm and 530–610 nm, respectively, which are usually regarded as extensive absorption on the surface of CQDs because more conjugated groups are attached to the edges [58].The different absorption bands in the low-energy region of the four types of CQDs indicate the different surface states of the four types of CQDs, among which the surface state effects are stronger for Y-CQDs and R-CQDs than for B-CQDs and G-CQDs, which is also consistent with the results of the previous characterization [23].Fig. 3a–d also shows that the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs are 400 and 475 nm, 430 and 530 nm, 490 and 580 nm, 560 and 645 nm, respectively. And also, they exhibit blue, green, yellow and red fluorescence under UV light, respectively. Subsequently, their optical properties were further investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the PL emission of B-CDs was excitation dependent. As the excitation wavelength increases from 360 nm to 440 nm, the emission wavelength of B-CQDs increases from 430 nm to 530 nm, while the emission intensity increases and then decreases, reaching an optimal excitation of 475 nm at 400 nm excitation. The PL of G-CQDs is also excitation-dependent. With the increase of excitation wavelength from 390 nm to 470 nm, the emission wavelength of G-CQDs increases from 508 nm to 543 nm, and the emission intensity also increases firstly and then decreases, and reaches the optimal excitation wavelength of 530 nm at the excitation of 430 nm. However, the PLs of Y-CQDs and R-CQDs also show excitation-independence, with the increase of the excitation wavelength, the emission intensity is also increased firstly and then decreased. The above results suggest that the nonuniform surface states of B-CQDs and G-CQDs provide multiple energies for photoluminescence, which may be attributed to less functionalized passivation of the surfaces [59,60].The fluorescence independence exhibited by Y-CQDs and R-CQDs may be due to the addition of o-phenylenediamine to make the structure of the CQDs more regular and homogeneous. The redshift of the fluorescence emission wavelength coincides with the redshift of the exciton absorption peaks, further suggesting that the size of the sp2 conjugation domains of the four CDs has changed [49].In addition to the above optical properties, the PLQYs of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs at the optimal excitation wavelengths were determined by the integrating sphere method, and the values were about 30.13 %, 36.84 %, 39.16 %, and 21.36 %, respectively. Moreover, time-resolved PL decay curves were measured for four CQDs. The results are shown in Fig. 3e–h and Table S4, and they can be fitted with a double-exponential formula, which includes a short-life component τ1 and a long-life component τ2, which are assigned to the recombination process in the core and surface states, respectively [61]. It is noteworthy that from B-CQDs to R-CQDs, the average lifetime first increases from 2.45 ns to 4.93 ns and then decreases to 1.95ns. Most notably, both Y-CQDs and R-CQDs show a significant increase in the ratio of τ2 compared to B-CQDs and G-CQDs, which suggests that the role of the surface states in the radiative lifetime of these CQDs increases with their PL redshift increases [62].Therefore, it can be inferred that the surface state has a strong influence on the emission wavelength redshift and the transition of electrons. The above results suggest that our CQDs have excellent and homogeneous optical properties, and their tunable PL emission can be attributed to the surface states, which are controlled by the incorporation of different nitrogen-containing substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible emission and formation mechanism of multicolor carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the analysis of the physicochemical structures and optical properties of the above four CQDs, we have reasoned about the tunable luminescence mechanism of CQDs. In the hydrothermal environment of high temperature and high pressure, tyrosine and nitrogen-containing compounds are dehydrated, carbonized, and cross-linked to form a large conjugated sp2 hybrid structure. For the four CQDs, the amino and carboxyl groups decomposed in the tyrosine structure and the amino groups in the added ethylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine amide each other in the high-temperature and high-pressure environments, forming a large rigid sp2 conjugated structure with C=O,-OH,-NH2 attached to its edges; for the R-CQDs it can be deduced that the addition of the acid promotes the amide reaction and makes the sp2 conjugated structure further expanded, and the optimal emission wavelength of CQDs was red-shifted. The possible formation mechanism diagrams of CQDs are shown in Fig. S3: (a) corresponding to B-CQDs and G-CQDs; (b) corresponding to Y-CQDs and R-CQDs. Furthermore, CQDs with different sp2 conjugation sizes were prepared by varying the feedstock in the reaction. We consider that the prepared CQDs are believed to have larger sp2 conjugated C-structures and fewer sp3 hybridized C-structures. The bandgap energies of the four CQDs are calculated as E
g
(opt) = 1240/λ
edge , where λ
edge is the wavelength corresponding to the maximum exciton absorption peak [9,63]. Theoretically calculated values are shown in Fig. S4 for 2.61 (B-CQDs), 2.34 (G-CQDs), 2.13 (Y-CQDs) and 1.92 eV (R-CQDs).

The bandgap energy decreases as the size of the CDs increases, indicating that the bandgap energy is size-dependent [63]. Larger sizes lead to π-electron delocalization in the sp2 domain resulting in a narrowing of the bandgap and a redshift of the emission wavelength. This is consistent with the results of earlier studies [19]. Referring to the previous TEM, FTIR, and XPS analyses of the structures, it is consistently shown that with the redshift of the emission wavelength, the particle sizes of the four CQDs gradually increase, and the OH, NH2, and C=O contents of the surfaces gradually increase. Moreover, with the redshift of the emission wavelength, the fluorescence lifetimes of the four CQDs generally show the transfer from the core state to the surface state, and thus it can be deduced that the tunability of the CQDs luminescence mechanism is synergistically controlled by the size of the sp2 conjugated structure and the surface state. The possible fluorescence mechanisms and energy levels of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs are schematically shown in Fig. 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of multicolor carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs play an irreplaceable role in many fields due to their remarkable structural advantages and optical properties. However, the fluorescence quenching resulting from excessive energy transfer between CQDs in the solid state also restricted their further research and applicability. Based on the extensive functional clusters on the surfaces of CQDs, we have tried two strategies to realize their solid-state luminescence. First, based on the enriched hydrophilic group on the surfaces of CQDs, we incorporated the four prepared CQDs into nonionic waterborne polyurethanes to obtain CQDs/WPU complex films. Pictures of the four types of complex films exposed to the solar and ultraviolet light are illustrated in Fig. 5 a–b. As can be seen from the figure, the four complex films have uniform clarity and exhibit homogeneous and bright solid fluorescence film of four colors under UV light. Second, the traditional CQDs-based composite phosphor usually adopts a complicated mixing method, we choose silica gel powder used in column chromatography as the dispersed matrix, which has a rich specific surface area and pore structure, and is also a high-quality adsorption material. The ethanol solution of the four prepared CQDs was mixed with 200 mesh silica gel powder to prepare the CQDs/silica gel powder composite phosphor. The photographs of the four phosphors under sunlight and UV lamps are shown in Fig. 5c–f and Fig. 5g–j. As can be seen from the figures, the four phosphors showed bright blue, green, yellow and red solid powder fluorescence under UV lamp. In addition, we used a 365 nm light-emitting microchip as an excitation source and coated it with CQDs/WPUs to obtain bright four-color LED with International Commission on Illumination (CIE 1931) coordinates of (0.21,0.31), (0.30,0.52), (0.51,0.49), and (0.68,0.29), respectively. The prepared monochromatic LEDs emission spectra and optical photographs are shown in Fig. 6 a–d and CIE coordinates are shown in Fig. 6f–i. Standard white LEDs (WLEDs) are obtained by mixing the spectral triad of CQDs in a certain ratio (Fig. 6e).As shown in Fig. 6j, the CIE coordinates of the resulting WLED was (0.32,0.32), the correlated color temperature (CCT) was 6143 K, and the color rendering index was 91.6, indicating that the method is suitable for fabricating standard WLED with high color rendering index.

In summary, a new strategy for the synthesis of tunable multicolor-emitting CQDs covering the entire visible spectral range is proposed. High-quality multicolor CQDs were prepared using different feedstock types under true hydrothermal conditions at 200 °C. Based on various characterization analyses, it is inferred that the primary reason for the red-shift in the emission wavelength is the synergistic effect of the sp2 conjugate size and the surface state. To realize the solid-state luminescence of CQDs, we successfully prepared multicolor CQDs/WPU solid-state film luminescence and multicolor CQDs/silica gel powder solid-state powder luminescence. Meanwhile, based on the excellent PL properties of CQDs, we successfully prepared bright monochromatic LEDs and WLEDs with CIE coordinates of (0.32,0.32), CCT of 6143 K, and CRI of 91.6. In this work, CQDs with excellent tunable fluorescence properties were developed, and LED lighting and solid-state fluorescence based on the CQDs showed good performance.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
Xingyuan Ma: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Jianfeng Li: Software, Writing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Fujian Bureau of Major Projects (2019H4018) funding

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.120296.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable full-color solid-state fluorescent carbon dots for light emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, J. Zheng, Y. Yang, X. Liu, J. Qiu, Y. Tian, Tunable full-color solid-state fluorescent carbon dots for light emitting diodes, Carbon 190 (2022) 22-31.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot based sensing platform for real-time imaging Cu(2+) distribution in plants and environment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lin, X. Huang, E. Kou, W. Cai, H. Zhang, X. Zhang, Y. Liu, W. Li, Y. Zheng, B. Lei, Carbon dot based sensing platform for real-time imaging Cu(2+) distribution in plants and environment, Biosens Bioelectron 219 (2022) 114848.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-signal fluorescent colorimetric tetracyclines sensor based on multicolor carbon dots as probes and smartphone-assisted visual assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Fan, Z.G. Wang, M. Su, X.T. Liu, S.G. Shen, J.X. Dong, A dual-signal fluorescent colorimetric tetracyclines sensor based on multicolor carbon dots as probes and smartphone-assisted visual assay, Anal Chim Acta 1247 (2023) 340843.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, S.B. Yu, J.S. Wei, H.M. Xiong, Full-Color Light-Emitting Carbon Dots with a Surface-State-Controlled Luminescence Mechanism, ACS Nano 10(1) (2016) 484-491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-fast solvent-free protocol remodels the large-scale synthesis of carbon dots for nucleolus-targeting and white light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, J. Hu, H. Wei, Z. Li, J. Liu, J. Zhang, S. Yang, Ultra-fast solvent-free protocol remodels the large-scale synthesis of carbon dots for nucleolus-targeting and white light-emitting diodes, J Colloid Interface Sci 649 (2023) 785-794.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reaction time-controlled synthesis of multicolor carbon dots for white light-emitting diodes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step large-scale fabricating aggregation-induced emission carbon dots with strong solid-state fluorescence emission
